attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic with ongoing fatigue and exercise intolerance since testing positive for COVID-19 four months prior. She had three vaccine doses before her COVID-19 illness. During her COVID-19 illness, she experienced five days of subjective fevers, sore throat, non-productive cough, exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, and myalgia. She has had ongoing fatigue and concentration difficulties that are impacting her work as a primary school teacher. She no longer goes jogging due to fatigue, which she previously enjoyed. She has been sleeping for roughly 10 hours per night and does not wake feeling well-rested. Her mood has been lower since her COVID-19 illness.

She is otherwise well and takes no regular medications.

Her vital signs are within normal limits. Her physical examination is unremarkable. She is RAT negative. A urinalysis and ECG are also unremarkable. Her FBC, UEC, LFT, CRP, ESR, iron studies, TFTs, HbA1C, and BNP have all returned normal results.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in her management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is most likely suffering from post-COVID-19 condition, colloquially known as 'long COVID'. Post-COVID-19 condition is a syndrome which can consist of a variety of symptoms, persisting longer than 12 weeks post-acute infection and symptoms not otherwise attributed to a comorbid condition. The most common symptoms in adults are shortness of breath, activity and exercise intolerance, fatigue, myalgia, arthralgia, low-grade fevers, headache, mood disturbances, and cognitive dysfunction.

In this patient, the history, exam, and laboratory findings do not indicate cardiac failure, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, anaemia, hypothyroidism, malignancy, infection, or a rheumatological cause of this patient's fatigue and activity intolerance.

Management of post-COVID-19 condition currently revolves around managing potentially contributing comorbidities, optimising lifestyle factors such as smoking and alcohol consumption, and rehabilitation to combat decreased activity and exercise tolerance. At the time of writing, there are no approved pharmacotherapies for post-COVID-19 condition.

Cognitive behavioural therapy can be beneficial in managing the psychological aspects of post-COVID-19 syndrome, such as low mood and concentration difficulties. It can help patients develop coping strategies and improve their quality of life. Furthermore, recent trials have shown that cognitive behavioural therapy has been effective in reducing fatigue for patients with post-COVID-19 condition. Of note, in some cases, an approach that also evaluates the emotional and cognitive effect of focused attention on symptoms and their specific meaning for the patient can be beneficial (in a similar way that CBT benefits patients with somatic symptom disorders). However, as with some other somatic symptom disorders, the patient's belief in the damaging meaning of their symptoms, the reinforcement of this belief by social media and some medical practitioners and the power of secondary gains (particularly adoption of the sick role and receipt of a disability pension) amongst other issues, also need to be considered. Hence one needs to move slowly, gently and with great diplomacy.

A watch and wait approach would not be appropriate. While post-COVID-19 condition can improve without intervention, and there is no pharmacological intervention available, this patient is suffering from activity and exercise intolerance and there is evidence that a rehabilitation program delivered by a physiotherapist can help.

Casirivimab plus imdevimab is the current recommendation for acute COVID-19 infection in patients with one or more serious comorbidity, who are at risk of deterioration. This patient does not have an acute COVID-19 infection as demonstrated by her negative RAT today.

Dexamethasone is currently recommended in adults with severe acute COVID-19 who require supplemental oxygen in hospital. It currently has no role in the management of post-COVID-19 syndrome.

Fluoxetine is a commonly prescribed selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor for major depressive disorder. While this patient does describe a low mood, the lack of additional depressive symptoms makes a diagnosis of major depressive disorder unlikely. Her low mood is most likely a symptom of her post-COVID-19 syndrome.

As a relatively new and poorly understood condition, evidence and recommendations surrounding post-COVID-19 are frequently updated. A link to the Australian living guidelines is available as a reference.

There has been some interest in the off-label use of naltrexone for long COVID, particularly at low doses, due to its potential anti-inflammatory effects. However, there is currently insufficient evidence to support its routine use in this context, and it remains an area of ongoing research.
learningPoints: There is currently no evidence for pharmacotherapy in long COVID.
Rehabilitation programs can help improve exercise tolerance in long COVID.
references: National Clinical Evidence Taskforce - COVID-19. Caring for People with COVID-19 Living Guidelines [Internet]. Canberra (AU): National Clinical Evidence Taskforce; 2022 [Updated 22 Oct 2022, cited 03 Nov 2022]. Available from: https://clinicalevidence.net.au/covid-19/
Ladlow P, Bennett AN, O'Sullivan O. Exercise Therapy for Post-COVID-19 Condition-Does No Harm. JAMA Netw Open. 2024 Apr 1;7(4):e246959. doi: 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2024.6959. PMID: 38573640.

Clin Infect Dis. 2023 Sep 11;77(5):687-695. doi: 10.1093/cid/ciad257. PMID: 37155736; PMCID: PMC10495128.
